capability.name: Research Synthesis
capability.description: Combine findings from multiple sources into coherent analysis. Identify agreement, contradiction, and gaps across sources. Produce structured summaries that distinguish established consensus from contested claims.

sets.research_question: Should we adopt server-side rendering (SSR) for our e-commerce site to improve SEO and performance?
sets.source_1: Source 1 (Google Web Dev blog, 2025): Core Web Vitals strongly correlate with search ranking. SSR consistently improves Largest Contentful Paint by 30-50% compared to client-side rendering. Google's crawler can now execute JavaScript, but SSR still provides more reliable indexing.
sets.source_2: Source 2 (Shopify engineering, 2024): After migrating from SSR to React Server Components, we saw a 20% reduction in server costs but a 15% increase in Time to First Byte. SEO impact was neutral — our pages were already well-indexed with CSR.
sets.source_3: Source 3 (Web Almanac survey, 2025): 68% of top-100 e-commerce sites use SSR or SSG. Sites using SSR had 22% better median LCP but 18% higher TTFB. No statistically significant difference in conversion rate was found between SSR and CSR sites with equivalent performance budgets.

sets.research_question: What's the most effective approach to error handling in distributed systems?
sets.source_1: Source 1 (Academic paper, 2024): Analysis of 500 production outages found that 62% were caused by error handling bugs, not the original errors. Retry storms caused 34% of cascading failures. Circuit breakers reduced cascade rate by 78%.
sets.source_2: Source 2 (Stripe engineering blog, 2025): We use idempotency keys for all write operations. Our retry policy is: exponential backoff with jitter, max 3 retries, and strict timeout budgets. This reduced duplicate charges by 99.7%.
sets.source_3: Source 3 (AWS Well-Architected Framework): Recommends retry with exponential backoff, circuit breakers for downstream dependencies, bulkheads for isolation, and structured error codes for observability. Notes that 'most teams over-retry and under-observe.'

criteria.description: Named important gaps not covered by any source
Produced an actionable synthesis, not just a list

sets.topic: whether 10x developers exist and how to hire them
sets.source_1: Source 1 (HBR article, 2024): 'Research shows top programmers are 10x more productive. Companies should invest heavily in identifying and retaining this rare talent.' Cites a 1968 study by Sackman et al.
sets.source_2: Source 2 (Tech recruiter blog, 2025): 'The 10x developer is real — our data shows top candidates outperform average hires by 8-12x on code output metrics.' No methodology disclosed.
sets.source_3: Source 3 (CTO conference talk, 2024): 'I've seen it firsthand — our best engineer does the work of ten average ones. The data backs this up.' References the same 1968 study.

sets.topic: the optimal sprint length for agile teams
sets.source_1: Source 1 (Scrum Guide): 'Sprints are fixed-length events of one month or less to create consistency. Most teams use two-week sprints.'
sets.source_2: Source 2 (State of Agile Report, 2025): '58% of surveyed teams use 2-week sprints. Teams report higher satisfaction with shorter sprints.'
sets.source_3: Source 3 (Agile coaching book): 'Two-week sprints are the gold standard. Start there and adjust only if you have a specific reason.'

criteria.description: Identified that sources are not truly independent
Explained why apparent agreement doesn't strengthen the claim
Described what genuine independent confirmation would look like
